title: Be Merciful, Judge Not
subtitle: On the mountainside: the Lord teaches mercy, the forbidding of judgment, and confidence in prayer

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of judging fall away.
The prayer of a just man pierces the heavens.
Grow quiet, and let your heart rise.
gospel: as your Father also is merciful.
and you shall not be judged.
and you shall not be condemned.
For with the same measure that you mete,
it shall be measured to you again.
behold: Attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold the Lord upon the mountain,
teaching the crowds that mercy belongs to God.
See how the Father relieves our miseries,
awaiting nothing from us in return,
moved only by His own goodness.
not in power, which the devil greedily craved,
and so was broken down into hell;
not in wisdom, which man coveted,
and so lost paradise, stripped of glory;
of which we wretched ones stand so greatly in need.
Behold your brother as another self,
made, as you are, to the image of God,
and take his grief of heart upon you as your own.
consider: Consider: it is natural for every living thing to keep mercy toward its own kind.
How much more must you, made to the image of God, have compassion on your fellow image.
Jerome says: I do not recall reading that anyone died an evil death who gladly did the works of mercy.
For he has many intercessors, and it is impossible that the prayers of many go unheard.
Then weigh the first work of mercy: not to judge.
Of many things it is uncertain with what mind they are done, for they may be done from a single heart or a double one.
Beware of being a prying searcher or a rash judge of another's life.
Even if you catch sight of something wrongly done — do not judge your brother; rather, excuse him.
Excuse his intention; and if you cannot excuse the deed, plead ignorance, plead surprise, plead mischance.
And if the plain certainty of the thing forbids all excuse, then say within yourself: the temptation was too violent for him;
what would it have done to me, if it had gained the same power over me?
Augustine warns: do not condemn the things you do not know with what mind they are done.
The evil interpret in the worst sense whatever they see or hear; the good read all in the better part, and so profit by everything.
Chrysostom strikes deeper: you do not condemn the sinner — you condemn yourself.
You make the judgment over you the more terrible, and force the most exacting vengeance to be drawn against you, even for the least things.
We sit as bitter and severe judges over the faults of others,
yet the beams fixed in our own eyes we do not see.
For this sin alone, were there no other, hell would be enough.
With what judgment you have judged, you shall be judged;
and with what measure you have measured, it shall be measured back to you.
prayer: who have taught us to show mercy to all, and to judge no one,
give me to fulfil what You teach,
that through You I may be able to please You.
You urge us to pray, and surely You will to give,
You who ever admonish us to ask.
I ask, then, because You command;
I knock, because You enjoin it.
You who made me ask, make me receive;
You who gave me to seek, grant me to find;
You who taught me to knock, open to the one who knocks, that he may enter in.
From You I have the desiring; from You may I have the obtaining.
Give what I may offer, guard what You require,
that You may will to crown what You bestow.

latinJewel.latin: Oratio enim viri justi cœlos penetrat; quomodo ergo in terris hostes non vincat?
latinJewel.english: For the prayer of a just man pierces the heavens; how then shall it not, on earth, conquer his enemies?
